---
title: "Turbines, Compressors, Pumps, Nozzles, Valves Reference Page<br/>*Entropy Made Me Do It*"
author: "DOFPro group"
format: html
html-math-method: mathjax
---
{{< include /LinkKey.qmd >}}
# Intro to Turbines, Compressors, Pumps, Nozzles, and Valves

Many important engineering devices convert energy between different forms of mechanical and thermal energy.  
Examples include turbines that generate power, compressors that increase gas pressure, pumps that move liquids, and nozzles that convert pressure into velocity.

The Second Law of Thermodynamics provides a way to measure how closely real equipment approaches ideal performance by comparing actual processes to **isentropic reference processes**.

## Entropy Made Me Do It: Turbines, Compressors, and Other Second-Law Shenanigans

This video explains how the Second Law is used to evaluate turbines and compressors using isentropic efficiency.

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Pb7PS5oeCz0?si=BSyZ_LWr9GLXmnVE" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>

[Visuals](118-TurbCompEtcVisuals.qmd)

## Entropy Made Me Do It: Pumps, Nozzles, and Other Second-Law Shenanigans 

This video extends the same ideas to pumps, nozzles, and throttling devices.

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/irBfMfVUEFo?si=HWUWastge-nrPBvP" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>

[Visuals](118.5-PumpNozzVisuals.qmd)

# Examples and Definitions

## Examples Links

## Definitions

Isentropic or Ideal Work  
: The work required (for devices that consume work) or produced (for devices that generate work) if the process were **isentropic**.

Real or Actual Work  
: The work actually required or produced by a real device.  
Actual work is compared with isentropic work to define device efficiencies.

[Turbine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbine) or [Expander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turboexpander)  
: A mechanical device that produces work from an expanding fluid.

Turbine or Expander Efficiency  

$$
\eta_\mathrm{turbine}
=
\frac{\dot W_\text{actual}}
{\dot W_\text{isentropic}}
$$

where the isentropic process has the same inlet state and outlet pressure as the real turbine.

[Adiabatic Compressor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compressor#Isentropic_compressor)  
: A device used to increase the pressure of a gas.  
Its performance is often evaluated by comparing the actual compression process to an isentropic compression.

Isentropic Compressor Efficiency  

$$
\eta_\mathrm{compressor}
=
\frac{\dot W_\text{isentropic}}
{\dot W_\text{actual}}
$$

[Isothermal Compressor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compressor#Temperature)  
: A compressor designed to remove heat during compression so that the gas temperature remains approximately constant.

Isothermal Compressor Efficiency  

$$
\eta_\mathrm{compressor}
=
\frac{\dot W_\text{isothermal}}
{\dot W_\text{actual}}
$$

[Pump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pump)  
: A device used to move liquids and increase their pressure.

Pump Efficiency  

$$
\eta_\mathrm{pump}
=
\frac{\dot W_\text{isentropic}}
{\dot W_\text{actual}}
$$

[Nozzle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nozzle)  
: A device that increases the velocity of a fluid by converting pressure energy into kinetic energy.

Nozzle Efficiency  

$$
\eta_\mathrm{nozzle}
=
\frac{u_\text{actual}^2}{u_\text{isentropic}^2}
$$

[Valve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Valve) or [Throttle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Throttle)  
: A device used to control or restrict fluid flow.  
Throttling devices are usually modeled as **isenthalpic processes**

$$
\Delta H = 0
$$

so that

$$
H_\mathrm{out} = H_\mathrm{in}
$$

# Other Links and Videos

## Other Links

## Previous and Following Videos

### Two videos back

**Sadi Carnot and the Power of Fire, the 1824 Edition Part 2**  
*The Steam Carnot Cycle*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/Qc9W1KKpouU), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/TheCarnotCycleVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/116.5-Visuals.qmd)

### Previous video

**How Much Entropy Can You Balance on the Head of a Pin (or in an Open Steady-State System)?**  
*The 2nd Law for Closed, Open Steady-State, and Open Transient Systems*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/QcZTezMtDRk), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/EntropyBalanceEquationsVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/117-2ndLawSystemsVisuals.qmd)

### Next video

**Boil, Expand, Condense, Repeat: The Rankine Cycle in Action Part 1**  
*The Ideal Rankine Cycle*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/4QRJ00uyaX8), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/RankineCycleInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/119-RankineVisualsPart1.qmd)

### Two videos forward

**Boil, Expand, Condense, Repeat: The Rankine Cycle in Action Part 2**  
*The Rankine Cycle with $\eta_\mathrm{pump}$ and $\eta_\mathrm{turbine}$*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/0DmsKCJpIdc), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/RankineCycleInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/120-RankineVisualsPart2.qmd)
